annotations.label: Algebra

anchor.snippet: the branch of mathematics that studies algebraic structures and the operations they use

annotations.note: The meta-concept of the discipline 'algebra' is not a formalizable mathematical object.

annotations.label: Algebraic structure

anchor.snippet: An algebraic structure is a non-empty set of mathematical objects

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general universal-algebra notion of 'an algebraic structure'; only specific classes (Group, Ring, etc.) exist.

annotations.label: Algebra (as a countable noun)

anchor.snippet: an algebra is a specific type of algebraic structure that involves a vector space

annotations.note: `Algebra R A` formalizes an algebra, though Mathlib's is associative unital over a commutative semiring.

annotations.label: Elementary algebra

anchor.snippet: is the oldest and most basic form of algebra

annotations.note: 'Elementary algebra' is an expository subject heading, not a formalizable object.

annotations.label: Arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Arithmetic is the study of numerical operations

annotations.note: 'Arithmetic' as a field of study is a meta-concept, not a single Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Addition combines addends into a sum

anchor.snippet: the operation of addition combines two numbers, called the addends

annotations.note: The addition operation is the core `Add`/`HAdd` typeclass underlying every additive structure in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Variables

anchor.snippet: Variables are symbols for unspecified or unknown quantities

annotations.note: Mathematical 'variables' as symbols are a syntactic notion, not a formalized object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Commutative property of multiplication

anchor.snippet: the commutative property of multiplication

annotations.note: `mul_comm` states `a * b = b * a`, the commutativity axiom of `CommMonoid`/`CommMagma`.

annotations.label: Algebraic expression

anchor.snippet: Algebraic expressions are formed by using arithmetic operations to combine variables and numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general 'algebraic expression' syntax object (polynomials are the closest specialized case).

annotations.label: Equation

anchor.snippet: An equation is a statement formed by comparing two expressions

annotations.note: Equations are expressed via the equality relation `Eq` (`=`), a core foundational notion used throughout Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inequation

anchor.snippet: Inequations involve a different type of comparison

annotations.note: Inequalities use the order relations `LT`/`LE` (`<`,`≤`) and `Ne` (`≠`), all foundational typeclasses.

annotations.label: Truth value depends on variables

anchor.snippet: their truth value usually depends on the values of the variables

annotations.note: This is an informal observation about open predicates, not a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Identity equations

anchor.snippet: Identity equations are true for all values that can be assigned to the variables

annotations.note: Universally-true equations are stated as `∀`-theorems but 'identity equation' is not a defined object.

annotations.label: Conditional equations

anchor.snippet: Conditional equations are only true for some values

annotations.note: 'Conditional equation' is an expository classification, not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Solving an equation

anchor.snippet: a process known as solving the equation for that variable

annotations.note: Solving is a procedural technique, not a formalized mathematical object.

annotations.label: Simplification

anchor.snippet: Simplification is employed to replace a complicated expression with an equivalent simpler one

annotations.note: Simplification is realized by the `simp` tactic, not a formalized mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Substitution

anchor.snippet: substitution is a common technique to replace one variable with an equivalent expression

annotations.note: Substitution is a proof/solving technique rather than a formalized object.

annotations.label: Graph of an equation

anchor.snippet: Algebraic equations can be interpreted geometrically to describe spatial figures

annotations.note: The geometric interpretation of an equation as a figure is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Polynomial

anchor.snippet: A polynomial is an expression consisting of one or more terms

annotations.note: `Polynomial R` (`R[X]`) is the univariate polynomial type over a semiring.

annotations.label: Monomial, binomial, trinomial

anchor.snippet: A monomial is a polynomial with one term

annotations.note: `Polynomial.monomial` formalizes a monomial, but binomial/trinomial are not separately named.

annotations.label: Degree of a polynomial

anchor.snippet: The degree of a polynomial is the maximal value

annotations.note: `Polynomial.degree` gives the polynomial degree as a `WithBot ℕ`.

annotations.label: Linear polynomial

anchor.snippet: Polynomials of degree one are called linear polynomials

annotations.note: Degree-one polynomials are expressible via `degree p = 1` but there is no standalone 'linear polynomial' type.

annotations.label: Univariate / multivariate polynomial

anchor.snippet: A polynomial is said to be univariate or multivariate

annotations.note: `Polynomial` is univariate and `MvPolynomial σ R` is the multivariate polynomial type.

annotations.label: Factorization

anchor.snippet: Factorization is a method used to simplify polynomials

annotations.note: Existence/uniqueness of factorization into irreducibles is formalized via `UniqueFactorizationMonoid`, but 'factorization as a simplification method' is informal.

anchors.snippet: Factorization is a method used to simplify polynomials

annotations.label: Zero product of factors

anchor.snippet: The polynomial as a whole is zero if and only if one of its factors is zero

annotations.note: `mul_eq_zero` states `a * b = 0 ↔ a = 0 ∨ b = 0` in a structure with no zero divisors.

anchors.snippet: The polynomial as a whole is zero if and only if one of its factors is zero

annotations.label: Quadratic formula

anchor.snippet: is given by the quadratic formula

annotations.note: `quadratic_eq_zero_iff` gives the roots `(-b ± s)/(2a)` where `s² = discriminant`.

anchors.snippet: is given by the quadratic formula

annotations.label: Cubic and quartic formulas

anchor.snippet: Solutions for the degrees 3 and 4 are given by the cubic and quartic formulas

annotations.note: No Cardano/Ferrari radical solution formula for cubics or quartics was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Abel–Ruffini theorem

anchor.snippet: There are no general solutions for higher degrees

annotations.note: Only the direction 'solvable by radicals ⇒ solvable Galois group' is proved; no explicit unsolvable quintic giving the full statement.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of algebra

anchor.snippet: asserts that every univariate polynomial equation of positive degree

annotations.note: `Complex.exists_root` proves every nonconstant complex polynomial has a root (also `Complex.isAlgClosed`).

annotations.label: Linear equation

anchor.snippet: An equation is linear if it can be expressed in the form

annotations.note: There is no dedicated 'linear equation' object; linearity is handled via linear maps and matrix equations.

annotations.label: System of linear equations

anchor.snippet: A system of linear equations is a set of linear equations

annotations.note: Systems are represented as matrix equations `A *ᵥ x = b` rather than via a dedicated 'linear system' type.

annotations.label: Matrix

anchor.snippet: Matrices are rectangular arrays of values

annotations.note: `Matrix m n α` is the type of `m × n` arrays indexed by `m` and `n`.

anchors.snippet: Matrices are rectangular arrays of values

annotations.label: Inconsistent system

anchor.snippet: Some systems of equations are inconsistent

annotations.note: No declaration captures the notion of an inconsistent linear system as such.

annotations.label: Consistent systems solutions

anchor.snippet: Consistent systems have either one unique solution or an infinite number of solutions

annotations.note: The 'one or infinitely many solutions' dichotomy for consistent systems is not stated as a single theorem.

annotations.label: Vector space

anchor.snippet: A vector space is an algebraic structure formed by a set with an addition

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `Module R M`; a vector space is a module over a field/division ring.

annotations.label: Linear map

anchor.snippet: A linear map is a function between vector spaces

annotations.note: `LinearMap` bundles additive, scalar-multiplication-preserving maps between modules.

annotations.label: Matrices and finite-dimensional vector spaces

anchor.snippet: the theories of matrices and finite-dimensional vector spaces are essentially the same

annotations.note: `LinearMap.toMatrix` is a linear equivalence between linear maps and matrices given bases.

annotations.label: Equations as lines in two-dimensional space

anchor.snippet: each equation represents a line in two-dimensional space

annotations.note: The interpretation of a linear equation as a line in the plane is not formalized as a statement.

annotations.label: Algebraic structure (formal)

anchor.snippet: called the underlying set, together with one or several operations

annotations.note: Mathlib has no generic universal-algebra 'structure with operations' object, only specific typeclasses.

annotations.label: Binary operation

anchor.snippet: which take any two objects from the underlying set as inputs

annotations.note: Binary operations are bundled by the core `Mul`/`Add` typeclasses (a set with `Mul` is a magma).

annotations.label: Natural numbers with addition

anchor.snippet: as the underlying set and addition

annotations.note: ℕ with addition is an `AddCommMonoid` instance in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetry group of a geometric object

anchor.snippet: the underlying set of the symmetry group of a geometric object

annotations.note: `Equiv.Perm` is the full symmetric group; symmetry groups of figures are subgroups of permutations/isometries.

annotations.label: Group

anchor.snippet: which has one operation and requires that this operation is associative

annotations.note: `Group` is a monoid with inverses, exactly matching the group axioms.

annotations.label: Associativity

anchor.snippet: An operation is associative if the order of several applications does not matter

annotations.note: `mul_assoc` states `a * b * c = a * (b * c)`, the `Semigroup` axiom.

annotations.label: Identity element

anchor.snippet: An operation has an identity element or a neutral element

annotations.note: `MulOneClass` provides an identity `1` with the axioms `one_mul`/`mul_one`.

annotations.label: Inverse elements

anchor.snippet: An operation has inverse elements if for any element

annotations.note: The existence of inverses is the defining axiom (`inv_mul_cancel`) of `Group`.

annotations.label: Integers form a group under addition

anchor.snippet: is a group formed by the set of integers together with the operation of addition

annotations.note: ℤ with addition is an `AddCommGroup` instance.

annotations.label: Natural numbers are not a group

anchor.snippet: The natural numbers with addition, by contrast, do not form a group

annotations.note: The negative claim that ℕ is not a group is not stated as a theorem (there is simply no group instance).

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of finite abelian groups / Feit–Thompson

anchor.snippet: basic theorems such as the fundamental theorem of finite abelian groups

annotations.note: The structure theorem of finite abelian groups is formalized, but the Feit–Thompson theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classification of finite simple groups

anchor.snippet: culminated in a complete classification of finite simple groups

annotations.note: The classification of finite simple groups is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ring

anchor.snippet: A ring is an algebraic structure with two operations

annotations.note: `Ring` is an additive abelian group with an associative distributive multiplication and unit.

annotations.label: Commutative ring

anchor.snippet: if it is commutative, one has a commutative ring

annotations.note: `CommRing` extends `Ring` with commutative multiplication.

annotations.label: Field

anchor.snippet: and each nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse

annotations.note: `Field` is a commutative division ring where every nonzero element is invertible.

annotations.label: Ring of integers is not a field

anchor.snippet: The ring of integers does not form a field

annotations.note: The negative claim that ℤ is not a field is not stated as a theorem (there is no field instance for ℤ).

annotations.label: Rationals, reals, complex numbers are fields

anchor.snippet: each form a field with the operations of addition and multiplication

annotations.note: Field instances exist for ℚ (`Rat.instField`), ℝ (`Real.instField`), and ℂ (`Complex.instField`).

annotations.label: Hilbert's basis theorem

anchor.snippet: theorems such as Hilbert's basis theorem

annotations.note: `Polynomial.isNoetherianRing` is the Hilbert basis theorem: a polynomial ring over a Noetherian ring is Noetherian.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of Galois theory

anchor.snippet: relying on the fundamental theorem of Galois theory

annotations.note: `IsGalois.intermediateFieldEquivSubgroup` gives the Galois correspondence for finite Galois extensions.

annotations.label: Magma becomes a semigroup

anchor.snippet: a magma becomes a semigroup if its operation is associative

annotations.note: `Semigroup` extends `Mul` (a magma) with the associativity axiom.

annotations.label: Homomorphism

anchor.snippet: A homomorphism is a function from the underlying set of one algebraic structure

annotations.note: Structure-preserving maps are bundled per structure, e.g. `MulHom`/`MonoidHom` (and `RingHom`, etc.).

annotations.label: Isomorphism

anchor.snippet: An isomorphism is a bijective homomorphism

annotations.note: `MulEquiv` (extending `Equiv`) is a bijective multiplicative homomorphism; analogous `RingEquiv` etc. exist.

annotations.label: Subalgebra

anchor.snippet: All operations in the subalgebra are required to be closed in its underlying set

annotations.note: The general substructure notion is realized by specific `Sub*` types; `Subalgebra` is the R-algebra case.

annotations.label: Even integers are a subalgebra

anchor.snippet: the set of even integers together with addition is a subalgebra

annotations.note: The even integers are `AddSubgroup.zmultiples (2 : ℤ)`, an additive subgroup of ℤ.

annotations.label: Universal algebra

anchor.snippet: Universal algebra is the study of algebraic structures in general

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain a general universal-algebra framework.

annotations.label: Identity (universal equation)

anchor.snippet: an identity is a universal equation or an equation that is true for all elements

annotations.note: The universal-algebra notion of an 'identity' is not formalized as an object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Variety

anchor.snippet: A variety is a class of all algebraic structures that satisfy certain identities

annotations.note: A variety in the universal-algebra sense is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Category

anchor.snippet: A category is a collection of objects together with a collection of morphisms

annotations.note: `CategoryTheory.Category` defines objects, morphisms, composition, and identities.

annotations.label: Equation describing a sphere

anchor.snippet: corresponds to a sphere in three-dimensional space

annotations.note: `Metric.sphere x r = {y | dist y x = r}` formalizes the sphere defined by the equation in Euclidean space.
